butter.note: Clarified butter, often used in Malaysian cooking.

flour.note: Commonly used in Malaysian desserts and gluten-free recipes.

sugar.note: Made from the sap of the palm tree, widely used in Malaysian desserts.

egg.note: Used as a vegan alternative in stir-fries and cakes.

soy sauce.note: A gluten-free alternative to traditional soy sauce.

fish sauce.note: A plant-based alternative with similar umami flavor.

chili.note: A smaller, spicier variety of chili used in Malaysian dishes.

garlic.note: Used as a milder, sweeter alternative in many Asian cuisines.

ginger.note: A root similar to ginger but with a more intense, citrusy flavor.

lemongrass.note: Provides a similar fresh citrus flavor.

coriander.note: Coriander leaves, widely used in Malaysian and Southeast Asian dishes.

turmeric.note: Contains similar spices like turmeric, often used in curries.

tamarind.note: Adds a sour and tangy flavor, often used in Malaysian soups.

palm oil.note: A neutral oil often used in Malaysian frying and cooking.

tofu.note: A fermented soybean product with a firmer texture.

coconut oil.note: Another commonly used oil in Southeast Asian cooking.

chili paste.note: A spicy, tangy paste used in many Malaysian dishes.

sweet soy sauce.note: A thick, sweet soy sauce commonly used in Malaysia and Indonesia.

star anise.note: Provides a similar sweet-spicy flavor in Malaysian stews.

mango.note: Another tropical fruit with a sweet and slightly tangy flavor.

banana leaves.note: Used for wrapping food in cooking; banana leaves add a unique flavor.
